data.transformedContent:
<current_datetime>2026-04-24T01:12:53.803Z</current_datetime>

I have a test solution with hundreds of tests. I need to separate integration tests from
unit tests for CI pipeline optimization. Here are samples from three projects — which ones
are integration tests and what indicators tell you that?

Project A (MSTest):
```csharp
[TestClass]
[TestCategory("Integration")]
public class PaymentGatewayTests
{
    [TestMethod]
    public async Task ChargeCard_ValidCard_ReturnsSuccess()
    {
        var client = new HttpClient();
        var gateway = new PaymentGateway(client);
        var result = await gateway.ChargeAsync("4111111111111111", 99.99m);
        Assert.IsTrue(result.Success);
    }
}
```

Project B (xUnit):
```csharp
public class OrderApiTests
{
    [Fact]
    [Trait("Category", "Integration")]
    public async Task CreateOrder_ReturnsCreatedStatus()
    {
        using var factory = new WebApplicationFactory<Program>();
        var client = factory.CreateClient();
        var response = await client.PostAsJsonAsync("/orders", new { Item = "Widget" });
        Assert.Equal(HttpStatusCode.Created, response.StatusCode);
    }
}
```

Project C (NUnit):
```csharp
[TestFixture]
public class DatabaseQueryTests
{
    [Test]
    public void GetCustomer_ById_ReturnsExpectedName()
    {
        using var conn = new SqlConnection("Server=localhost;...");
        conn.Open();
        var name = conn.QuerySingle<string>("SELECT Name FROM Customers WHERE Id = 1");
        Assert.That(name, Is.EqualTo("Alice"));
    }
}
```

data.content:
**All three are integration tests.** Here's how to tell:

---

### Project A (MSTest) — Integration
| Indicator | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Explicit category** | `[TestCategory("Integration")]` |
| **Real HTTP client** | `new HttpClient()` — not mocked |
| **External dependency** | Calls a payment gateway (network I/O) |

### Project B (xUnit) — Integration
| Indicator | Detail |
|---|---|
| **Explicit trait** | `[Trait("Category", "Integration")]` |
| **In-process server** | `WebApplicationFactory<Program>` spins up the full ASP.NET pipeline |
| **HTTP round-trip** | `PostAsJsonAsync` makes a real HTTP call |

### Project C (NUnit) — Integration ⚠️
| Indicator | Detail |
|---|---|
| **No category tag** | This one is **unmarked** — easy to miss in CI filtering |
| **Real database** | `new SqlConnection(...)` hitting `localhost` |
| **Infrastructure dependency** | Requires a running SQL Server instance |

---

### Key indicators to scan for at scale

```
# Explicit markers (filterable)
[TestCategory("Integration")]          # MSTest
[Trait("Category", "Integration")]     # xUnit
[Category("Integration")]             # NUnit

# Implicit markers (require code inspection)
new HttpClient()                       # network calls
WebApplicationFactory<>                # full app bootstrap
SqlConnection / DbContext              # database access
new SmtpClient()                       # email
Process.Start()                        # external processes
```

### CI filtering recommendation

For Projects A and B, you can filter immediately:

```bash
# Run only unit tests (exclude integration)
dotnet test --filter "TestCategory!=Integration"          # MSTest
dotnet test --filter "Category!=Integration"              # xUnit
dotnet test --filter "TestCategory!=Integration"          # NUnit
```

**Project C is the risk** — it has no category attribute, so it will run with your unit tests and fail if no database is available. Tag it with `[Category("Integration")]` first, then the filter will catch it too.
data.reasoningText: The user is asking me to analyze three test code samples and identify which are integration tests and what indicators reveal that. This is a straightforward analysis question — no tools needed.
